en.extract:
The blue-capped ifrit (Ifrita kowaldi) is a species of small passerine bird in the monotypic family, Ifritidae, native to the montane rainforests of New Guinea. These birds are insectivorous and are notable for their distinctive blue crown and presence of batrachotoxin, a potent neurotoxin, in their feathers and skin. This chemical defense is believed to come from their diet, particularly Choresine beetles. Despite their toxicity, blue-capped ifrits are non-aggressive and typically found in small groups within high altitude forest habitats. Although they are currently listed as Least concern by the IUCN, their population is slowly declining due to habitat disturbance and limited conservation measures.


Description
The blue-capped ifrit is 16–17 cm (6.5–6.5 in) long and weighs 34–36 g (1.2–1.3 oz). The bird shows brown upperparts (top side of the bird including head, back, wings, and tail) with finely dotted wing edges and light-yellow underparts (the underside of bird, including belly, throat, chest, and under the wings and tail). 
The head is marked with a bright blue cap outlined in black, a small black spot on the crown, and a long white stripe running besides the eye. A thin black strip is within this strip, and the eye itself is encircled by a narrow white ring. 
Ifrits tend to have more stout body shapes with broad chests. Their wings are short and rounded while their legs are booted, having feathers down to their stout and clawed feet.
The species is sexually dimorphic, with ear streak coloration being white in males and more tawny yellow in females. Additionally, males have darker markings around the eyes and have more vivid coloration on the head and neck, females have paler markings around the eyes, and juveniles resemble females but have a light brown forehead.


Taxonomy
The blue-capped ifrit (Ifrita kowaldi) is the sole member of the family Ifritidae, making the family monotypic.The bird belongs to the order Passeriformes and the superfamily Corvoidea, a large group that also includes crows, birds of paradise, and jays.
The species (Ifrita kowaldi) was first described by Charles Walter De Vis (1890), while the genus Ifrita was discovered by Walter Rothschild in 1898.


Taxonomic history and evolution
Previously, the ifrit has been placed in a plethora of families including Cinclosomatidae or Monarchidae. Blue-capped ifrits are considered an ancient relict species endemic to New Guinea. This corvoid species originally dates back to the Oligocene epoch, on a series of proto-Papuan islands, with minimal known evolutionary divergences.


Common names
The blue-capped ifrit is known a variety names in different languages.

English: Blue-capped Ifrit / Blue-capped Ifrita / Ifrit
German: Blaukappenflöter
Dutch: Blauwkapifrita
Danish: Blåkrone
Norwegian: Blåkroneifrit
Spanish: Ifrita
Italian: Ifrita


Habitat

Blue-capped ifrits are endemic to Western New Guinea and Papua New Guinea, being found nowhere else on earth, with a distribution of 388,000 km2. They occur in both countries including the Southern Highlands, Western Highlands, Enga, Morobe, and Eastern Highland provinces. 
The species mainly lives in subtropical and tropical moist montane forests and is highly dependent on the forest ecosystem. It is found from roughly 100 to 4,000 meters (210- 12,070 ft) in elevation, though it is most commonly found at altitudes of 1,500 meters (4,900 feet) or above.


Toxicity

The blue-capped ifrit is one of the few birds that accumulate batrachotoxin (BTX) in their feathers and skin. This potent neurotoxin is thought to be deadlier than cyanide and causes sneezing, irritation, and allergic reactions in humans who handle the bird.


Source of the toxin
Batrachotoxin is thought to enter the Ifrit's body through its diet, most likely from Chroesine beetles from the Melyridae family. Some researchers believe these beetles may gain the toxin from soil mites or certain plants. The chemical provides the bird with strong protection against predators and parasites. Levels of BTX can vary between species and individuals, but the blue-capped ifrit and the hooded pitohui (Pitohui dichrous) showed the highest concentrations. In 2023, scientists discovered two additional toxic bird species from Papua New Guinea, highlighting how little is still known about toxic avians.


Mechanism, effects, and adaptations
Batrachotoxin (BTX) works by holding the sodium ion channels in muscle and nerve cells open, which can cause muscle paralysis and potentially death. The blue-capped ifrit, however, is resistant to its own toxin because of a mutation in the SCN4A gene which codes for the NAV1.4 sodium channel that helps muscles work. This mutation changes the channel's shape so the BTX can't bind properly, explained by target-site insensitivity. Similar mutations are found in poison dart frogs, providing an example of convergent evolution.
Some studies suggest that the sodium channels remain partly sensitive, so scientists believe that the bird may also produce a protein that "soaks up the poison" safely. Additionally, because the blue-capped ifrit have to move BTX from its stomach to its feather and skin, researchers believe there is a transporter protein that distributes it.


Behavior


Mating
Nesting occurs from August to November. Nests are built from green moss and leaf fern and are usually placed 2-3 meters (6.5-10 ft) above the ground, often in a fork if a small tree or sapling. Each clutch typically contains a single white egg with black and purple spots for camouflage. Both parents participate in nest building and chick care, and the small clutch size points to a high investment in the offspring (k-selected species). The birds' toxin-covered breast and belly feathers may rub off on the egg or nest, which offers chemical protection from predators.


Vocalization
The blue-capped ifrit's song produces an upbeat "toowa-tee!", along with a short dry "Wik" call.


Diet
The species primarily feeds on insects and worms, which indicate insectivorous feeding behaviors. They forage carefully through moss, branches, and tree trunks. Occasionally, the bird will eat soft fruits to supplement a food source. The blue-capped ifrit's toxicity is gained though their diet, mostly from Choresine beetles of the Melyridae family.


Social and territorial behavior
Blue-capped ifrits are often seen in small groups of up to six, suggesting social behavior. The birds are rarely seen alone, which may indicate that are not territorial.


Conservation status
Ifrits are of least concern because they have a large range and have relatively large population. Some of their habitat is within conservation sites. Although the birds are toxic, they have not been found in bird markets or sold online in Indonesia, unlike the pitohuis. The species is not officially protected by Indonesian law, because the trade is unregulated, so they could be sold illegally. While the population is slowly decreasing, the decline is not fast enough for the species to be considered vulnerable. However, data shows that tree cover in their habitat has declined by 1.7% over the past decade, which has contributed to a population decline of up to 19%.


See also
Batrachotoxin
Toxic bird


References


Bibliography
del Hoyo, J.; Elliot, A.; Christie, D., eds. (2007). Handbook of the Birds of the World. Vol. 12: Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees. Lynx Edicions. ISBN 978-84-96553-42-2.


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet
The Intoxicating Birds of New Guinea by John Tidwell

Least concern by the IUCN, their population is slowly declining due to habitat disturbance and limited conservation measures.<ref name="Boles_2020" /><ref name="BirdLife DataZone" />

==Description==
The blue-capped ifrit is {{convert|16|–|17|cm|in|round=0.5|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|34|-|36|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Boles_2020" /> The bird shows brown upperparts (top side of the bird including head, back, wings, and tail) with finely dotted wing edges and light-yellow underparts (the underside of bird, including belly, throat, chest, and under the wings and tail).<ref name="ebird.org" /> 

The head is marked with a bright blue cap outlined in black, a small black spot on the crown, and a long white stripe running besides the eye. A thin black strip is within this strip, and the eye itself is encircled by a narrow white ring.<ref name="ebird.org" /> 

Ifrits tend to have more stout body shapes with broad chests. Their wings are short and rounded while their legs are booted, having feathers down to their stout and clawed feet.<ref name="Schodde_2014" />

The species is [[Sexual dimorphism|sexually dimorphic]], with ear streak coloration being white in males and more tawny yellow in females.<ref name="Schodde_2014" /> Additionally, males have darker markings around the eyes and have more vivid coloration on the head and neck, females have paler markings around the eyes, and juveniles resemble females but have a light brown forehead.<ref name="Boles_2020" />

== Taxonomy ==
The blue-capped ifrit (''Ifrita kowaldi)'' is the sole member of the family Ifritidae, making the family monotypic.<ref name="Schodde_2014" /><ref name="afo.birdlife.org.au" />The bird belongs to the order Passeriformes and the superfamily [[Corvoidea]], a large group that also includes crows, birds of paradise, and jays.<ref>{{Cite web |date=11 June 2025 |title=AviList: A Unified Global Checklist of the World's Birds is Now Available |url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/home/avilist-unified-global-checklist/ |access-date=18 September 2025 |website=Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology |language=en-US |archive-date=6 September 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250906214149/https://www.birds.cornell.edu/home/avilist-unified-global-checklist/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="www.gbif.org">{{Cite web |title=Ifrita kowaldi (De Vis, 1890) |url=https://www.gbif.org/species/2495069 |access-date=18 September 2025 |website=www.gbif.org |language=en |archive-date=22 February 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250222022633/https://www.gbif.org/species/2495069 |url-status=live }}</ref>

The species (''Ifrita kowaldi)'' was first described by [[Charles Walter De Vis]] (1890), while the genus ''Ifrita'' was discovered by [[Walter Rothschild, 2nd Baron Rothschild|Walter Rothschild]] in 1898.<ref name="www.gbif.org" />

=== Taxonomic history and evolution ===
Previously, the ifrit has been placed in a plethora of families including [[Cinclosomatidae]] or [[Monarchidae]]. Blue-capped ifrits are considered an ancient relict species endemic to [[New Guinea]]. This [[Corvoidea|corvoid]] species originally dates back to the [[Oligocene]] epoch, on a series of proto-Papuan islands, with minimal known evolutionary divergences.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jønsson |first1=Knud Andreas |last2=Borregaard |first2=Michael Krabbe |last3=Carstensen |first3=Daniel Wisbech |last4=Hansen |first4=Louis A. |last5=Kennedy |first5=Jonathan D. |last6=Machac |first6=Antonin |last7=Marki |first7=Petter Zahl |last8=Fjeldså |first8=Jon |last9=Rahbek |first9=Carsten |date=2 November 2017 |title=Biogeography and Biotic Assembly of Indo-Pacific Corvoid Passerine Birds |url=https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-ecolsys-110316-022813 |journal=Annual Review of Ecology, Evolution, and Systematics |language=en |volume=48 |issue=<!--none--> |pages=231–253 |doi=10.1146/annurev-ecolsys-110316-022813 |issn=1543-592X |archive-date=24 April 2025 |access-date=7 October 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250424132144/https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-ecolsys-110316-022813 |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Common names ===
The blue-capped ifrit is known a variety names in different languages.<ref name="www.gbif.org" />

* {{langx|en|Blue-capped Ifrit / Blue-capped Ifrita / Ifrit}}
* {{langx|de|Blaukappenflöter}}
* {{langx|nl|Blauwkapifrita}}
* {{langx|da|Blåkrone}}
* {{langx|no|Blåkroneifrit}}
* {{langx|es|Ifrita}}
* {{langx|it|Ifrita}}

==Habitat==
[[File:Papua_New_Guinea_(5986605253).jpg|thumb|[[Highland Papua|Highlands]] in New Guinea]]
Blue-capped ifrits are endemic to [[Western New Guinea]] and [[Papua New Guinea]], being found nowhere else on earth, with a distribution of 388,000 km<sup>2</sup>.<ref name="Bird Life International" /> They occur in both countries including the [[Southern Highlands Province|Southern Highlands]], [[Western Highlands Province|Western Highlands]], [[Enga Province|Enga]], [[Morobe Province|Morobe]], and [[Eastern Highlands Province|Eastern Highland provinces]].<ref name="ebird.org" /><ref name="BirdLife DataZone" /> 

The species mainly lives in subtropical and tropical moist montane forests and is highly dependent on the forest ecosystem.<ref name="BirdLife DataZone" /> It is found from roughly 100 to 4,000 meters (210- 12,070 ft) in elevation, though it is most commonly found at altitudes of {{convert|1,500|m|ft|abbr=off|sp=us}} or above.<ref name="Donaghey_2015" /><ref name="BirdLife DataZone" />

== Toxicity ==
[[File:Batrachotoxin_skeletal.svg|thumb|Batrachotoxin molecule]]
The blue-capped ifrit is one of the few birds that accumulate [[Batrachotoxin|batrachotoxin (BTX)]] in their feathers and skin.<ref name="Zaaijer_2024" /><ref name="Bodawatta_2024" /><ref name="Nijman_2024" /> This potent neurotoxin is thought to be deadlier than [[Cyanide poisoning|cyanide]] and causes sneezing, irritation, and allergic reactions in humans who handle the bird.<ref name="Nijman_2024" /><ref name="Ong_2025" />

=== Source of the toxin ===
Batrachotoxin is thought to enter the Ifrit's body through its diet, most likely from [[Choresine|Chroesine]] beetles from the [[Melyridae]] family.<ref name="Zaaijer_2024" /><ref name="Bodawatta_2024" /><ref name="Nijman_2024" /><ref name="Ong_2025" /><ref name="Dumbacher_2004" /> Some researchers believe these beetles may gain the toxin from soil mites or certain plants.<ref name="Ong_2025" /> The chemical provides the bird with strong protection against predators and parasites.<ref name="Zaaijer_2024" /> Levels of BTX can vary between species and individuals, but the blue-capped ifrit and the [[Hooded pitohui|hooded pitohui ''(Pitohui dichrous)'']] showed the highest concentrations.<ref name="Bodawatta_2024" /> In 2023, scientists discovered two additional toxic bird species from Papua New Guinea, highlighting how little is still known about toxic avians.<ref name="Ong_2025" /><ref name="Dumbacher_2004" />

=== Mechanism, effects, and adaptations ===
Batrachotoxin (BTX) works by holding the sodium ion channels in muscle and nerve cells open, which can cause muscle paralysis and potentially death.<ref name="Bodawatta_2024" /><ref name="Nijman_2024" /><ref name="Ong_2025" /> The blue-capped ifrit, however, is resistant to its own toxin because of a mutation in the [[SCN4A (gene)|SCN4A gene]] which codes for the [[Nav1.4|NAV1.4]] sodium channel that helps muscles work. This mutation changes the channel's shape so the BTX can't bind properly, explained by '''target-site insensitivity.''' Similar mutations are found in poison dart frogs, providing an example of [[convergent evolution]].<ref name="Zaaijer_2024" /><ref name="Bodawatta_2024" /><ref name="Ong_2025" />

Some studies suggest that the sodium channels remain partly sensitive, so scientists believe that the bird may also produce a protein that "soaks up the poison" safely.<ref name="Ong_2025" /> Additionally, because the blue-capped ifrit have to move BTX from its stomach to its feather and skin, researchers believe there is a transporter protein that distributes it.<ref name="Ong_2025" />

== Behavior ==

=== Mating ===
Nesting occurs from August to November. Nests are built from green moss and leaf fern and are usually placed 2-3 meters (6.5-10 ft) above the ground, often in a fork if a small tree or sapling.<ref name="Boles_2020" /><ref name="Dumbacher_2000" /> Each [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] typically contains a single white egg with black and purple spots for camouflage.<ref name="Boles_2020" /> Both parents participate in nest building and chick care, and the small clutch size points to a high investment in the offspring ([[R/K selection theory|k-selected species]]).<ref name="Boles_2020" /> The birds' toxin-covered breast and belly feathers may rub off on the egg or nest, which offers chemical protection from predators.<ref name="Dumbacher_2000" />

=== Vocalization ===
The blue-capped ifrit's song produces an upbeat "toowa-tee!", along with a short dry "Wik" call.<ref name="ebird.org" />

=== Diet ===
The species primarily feeds on insects and worms, which indicate [[Insectivore|insectivorous]] feeding behaviors.<ref name="Boles_2020" /><ref name="ebird.org" /><ref name="Dumbacher_2000" /> They forage carefully through moss, branches, and tree trunks.<ref name="Dumbacher_2000" /> Occasionally, the bird will eat soft fruits to supplement a food source.<ref name="Boles_2020" /> The blue-capped ifrit's toxicity is gained though their diet, mostly from Choresine beetles of the Melyridae family.<ref name="Zaaijer_2024" /><ref name="Bodawatta_2024" /><ref name="Nijman_2024" /><ref name="Ong_2025" /><ref name="Dumbacher_2004" />

=== Social and territorial behavior ===
Blue-capped ifrits are often seen in small groups of up to six, suggesting social behavior. The birds are rarely seen alone, which may indicate that are not territorial.<ref name="Dumbacher_2000" />
== Conservation status ==
Ifrits are of least concern because they have a large range and have relatively large population.<ref name="Boles_2020" /> Some of their habitat is within conservation sites.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=BirdLife International (BirdLife International) |date=12 June 2024 |title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Ifrita kowaldi |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22705379/264289002 |journal=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250429234020/https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22705379/264289002 |archive-date=29 April 2025 |access-date=7 October 2025 |url-status=live }}</ref> Although the birds are toxic, they have not been found in bird markets or sold online in Indonesia, unlike the pitohuis. The species is not officially protected by Indonesian law, because the trade is unregulated, so they could be sold illegally.<ref name="Nijman_2024" /> While the population is slowly decreasing, the decline is not fast enough for the species to be considered vulnerable.<ref name="BirdLife DataZone" /> However, data shows that tree cover in their habitat has declined by 1.7% over the past decade, which has contributed to a population decline of up to 19%.<ref name="BirdLife DataZone" />

zh.extract:
蓝顶鹛鸫（学名：Ifrita kowaldi），又称藍冠鶥鶇，是雀形目鹛鸫科的唯一一种鸟类。
蓝顶鹛鸫体重约32.1克，翼长约79.8毫米，嘴峰长约16.3毫米，喙宽度约4.1毫米，喙厚度约4.7毫米，跗蹠长约26.8毫米，尾长约54.2毫米。蓝顶鹛鸫在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。


亚种
I. k. brunnea：分布于新几内亚中西部的山地。
I. k. kowaldi：分布于新几内亚中央高地和休恩半岛的山区。
